data.title: ModivCare, Inc. Class Action Lawsuit Alert: Johnson Fistel Announces Shareholders with Losses Should Contact the Law Firm for More Information
data.description:
ModivCare, Inc. Class Action Lawsuit Alert: Johnson Fistel Announces Shareholders with Losses Should Contact the Law Firm for More Information

Johnson Fistel, LLP announces that a class action lawsuit has commenced on behalf of investors of ModivCare, Inc. (NASDAQ: MODV). The lawsuit seeks to recover losses on behalf of investors who acquired securities between November 3, 2022, and September 15, 2024. Investors have until March 31, 2025, to apply to the Court to be appointed as lead plaintiff in the lawsuit.

If you incurred significant losses and want to act as the lead plaintiff in theModivCare, Inc. class action lawsuit or determine if you are eligible to receive a potential recovery of your losses, please submit your details here: https://www.johnsonfistel.com/investigations/modivcare-inc

Contact for More Information: James Baker, (619) 814-4471, jimb@johnsonfistel.com or fjohnson@johnsonfistel.com

The Class Action alleges that, during the Class Period, Defendants made misleading statements and omissions regarding the Company's business, financial condition, and prospects. Specifically, Defendants misled the market regarding the ability of its contracts to stabilize cash flow.

As the truth about ModivCare's business reached the market, the price of ModivCare's stock suffered significant declines, harming investors. For example, on September 16, 2024, before market hours, ModivCare filed a press release on a Form 8-K with the Securities and Exchange Commission, titled "Modivcare Provides Financial Update." Therein, the Company revised its 2024 Adjusted EBITDA guidance range from $185-$195 million to $170-$180 million, "primarily due to NEMT segment pricing accommodations made to strategically retain and expand key customer relationships." On this news, the Company's stock price fell $1.40 or nearly 10%, from $14.12 per share on September 15, 2024, to close at $12.72 per share on September 16, 2024, on unusually high trading volume.

Investor Action Steps: Shareholders who incurred losses during the class period, have until March 31, 2025, to move the court to become a lead plaintiff in this action.A lead plaintiff will act on behalf of all other class members in directing the class-action lawsuit. The lead plaintiff can select a law firm of its choice to litigate the class-action lawsuit. An investor's ability to share any potential future recovery of the class action lawsuit is not dependent upon serving as lead plaintiff.

About Johnson Fistel, LLP | Top Law Firm, Securities Fraud, Investors Rights:

Johnson Fistel, LLP is a nationally recognized shareholder rights law firm with offices in California, New York, Georgia, and Colorado. The firm represents individual and institutional investors in shareholder derivative and securities class action lawsuits. We also extend our services to foreign investors who have purchased on US exchanges. Stay updated with news on stock drops and learn how Johnson Fistel, LLP can help you recover your losses. For more information about the firm and its attorneys, please visit http://www.johnsonfistel.com.

Attorney advertising.
Past results do not guarantee future outcomes.
Services may be performed by attorneys in any of our offices.

Johnson Fistel, LLP has paid for the dissemination of this promotional communication, and Frank J. Johnson is the attorney responsible for its content.

Johnson Fistel, LLP
501 W. Broadway, Suite 800, San Diego, CA 92101
James Baker, Investor Relations or Frank J. Johnson, Esq., (619) 814-4471
jimb@johnsonfistel.com or fjohnson@johnsonfistel.com

data.title: 10-, 30-year yields head for first rise in four sessions on modest selloff
data.description:
By Vivien Lou Chen and Steve Goldstein

A modest selloff in long-dated government debt remained mostly intact Thursday morning after weekly jobless claims came in slightly higher than expected.

What's happening

-- The yield on the 2-year Treasury BX:TMUBMUSD02Y was 4.214%, up 3.1 basis points from 4.183% on Wednesday. Yields move in the opposite direction to prices.

-- The yield on the 10-year Treasury BX:TMUBMUSD10Y was 4.441%, up 2 basis points from 4.421% on Wednesday.

-- The yield on the 30-year Treasury BX:TMUBMUSD30Y was 4.649%, up less than 1 basis point from 4.642% on Wednesday.

-- Ten- and 30-year yields finished Wednesday with a third straight session of declines.

What's driving markets

Data released on Thursday showed that initial jobless-benefit claims rose slightly, by 11,000, to total 219,000 for the seven days that ended on Feb. 1, and they remained at a level that signals the labor market is still quite healthy. Economists polled by the Wall Street Journal had expected 214,000 claims.

Separately, the Labor Department said that U.S. productivity decelerated to a 1.2% annual growth rate during the fourth quarter, down from a 2.3% rise in the prior three-month period.

Beyond U.S. data, financial-market participants were weighing comments made by Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent on Wednesday. Bessent said in an interview with Fox Business Network that President Donald Trump was focused on the 10-year Treasury yield and not on the Federal Reserve's main policy-rate target to bring down borrowing costs. Bessent spoke after the Treasury refunding plan kept auction sizes little changed.

The view articulated by Bessent was "important news that shows the Trump administration's attitude to monetary policy and yields," said a Deutsche Bank team that included strategist Jim Reid.

-Vivien Lou Chen -Steve Goldstein

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  02-06-25 0947ET

data.title: Intrust IT of Cincinnati, Ohio Acquires the IT Support Division of Entegrity Consulting Group in Dayton, Ohio
data.description:
Intrust IT of Cincinnati, Ohio Acquires the IT Support Division of Entegrity Consulting Group in Dayton, Ohio

Intrust IT of Cincinnati, Ohio Acquires the IT Support Division of Entegrity Consulting Group in Dayton, Ohio

PR Newswire

CINCINNATI, Feb. 6, 2025

CINCINNATI, Feb. 6, 2025 /PRNewswire/ -- Effective January 1st, 2025, Intrust IT of Cincinnati, Ohio, proudly completed the acquisition of the IT support division of Entegrity Consulting Group of Dayton, Ohio. This strategic growth enhances our presence in the Cincinnati and Dayton regions, expanding our ability to deliver comprehensive managed IT services. Our employee-owners are eager to collaborate with our new clients, driving their business success by fostering stable, secure, and highly productive IT environments.

"Our employee-owners at Intrust IT are excited to bring the clients of Entegrity, along with their long-time employee Ben Schubert, into our portfolio of companies. It's a great opportunity to connect with new businesses while also continuing our commitment to expand into the Dayton, Ohio area," - Chad Adams, President of Intrust IT.

About Intrust IT:
Securing today and a better tomorrow since 1992 for our employee-owners, clients, and communities.

Intrust IT was founded in 1992 as an IT support, cybersecurity protection, and cloud computing partner that gets you and gets back to you. As a locally owned small business located in Cincinnati with offices in Dayton and Columbus, OH, we empathize with business owners, use our ingenuity to solve problems and deliver IT services that make our clients happy.

Curious and forward-thinking, our founder and CEO, Tim Rettig, was bold enough to open his company's books to employees and completely upend the typical corporate structure. With his orange suit and matching orange Oxfords, Tim's focus on openness and transparency led to hyper-client-focused employees and a winning culture. As of 2019, we are an employee-owned company. By growing together as a business, we inspire each other and our clients to do more and impact our region in a new way. We're proud to have been named to Inc. Magazine's Best Workplaces List and Best Places to Work by the Cincinnati Business Courier on multiple occasions. We strive to build a better future for communities worldwide as we give back. As a business, we've joined other early-stage companies to give back in the Pledge 1% movement, globally recognized as one of Fast Company's World's Most Innovative Companies and #1 Nonprofit. A partnership with us means you're helping to contribute to a better tomorrow.

www.Intrust-IT.com | 513-469-6500

View original content:https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/intrust-it-of-cincinnati-ohio-acquires-the-it-support-division-of-entegrity-consulting-group-in-dayton-ohio-302370256.html

SOURCE Intrust IT

data.title: The stock market is climbing a 'wall of worry' with no trouble this year - but can it continue?
data.description:
By Joseph Adinolfi

Investors are pouring money into U.S. equity funds, and buying bullish call options like they are going out of style

A tariff tantrum. DeepSeek's deep impact. The Philadelphia Eagles' return to the Super Bowl.

Investors have seen plenty to put them on edge to start 2025. The Federal Reserve's decision to keep further interest-rate cuts on hold and a batch of disappointing Big Tech earnings that contributed to Wednesday's 7.3% drop in shares of Google parent Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL) - it's largest daily decline in more than a year - can be added to that list.

Yet, despite these challenges, the S&P 500 SPX has marched higher. The index rose to 6,061.48 on Wednesday, after reversing an earlier decline. That brought it back to within 1 percentage point of a fresh record high.

This resilience of stocks in the face of adversity has helped to boost investors' confidence that they could be destined for another year of generous returns in 2025, as the roaring bull run that first took hold in late 2022 stretches into its third year.

Climbing the 'wall of worry'

But beneath the surface, things look somewhat more muddled.

Traders have been aggressively buying bullish call options tied to U.S. stocks, exchange-traded funds and indexes. Investment flows show money has continued to pour into U.S. equity funds.

At the same time, individual investors appear to be growing more cautious, according to one closely watched sentiment gauge.

The latest AAII survey, released Thursday, showed the percentage of respondents who expected stocks to climb over the next six months retreated to 33.3%, below its long-term average.

According to one market strategist, a little bit of apprehension could be a good thing. It suggests investors aren't getting complacent, and that there might be more dry powder ready to deploy into the market.

"It shows that markets are climbing walls of worry, so I think that is a good sign," said Mona Mahajan, senior market strategist at Edward Jones, about the AAII readings.

Meanwhile, the Cboe Total Put-Call Ratio, which compares trading volume in bearish put option contracts to activity in bullish call option contracts tied to equity indexes, ETFs and individual shares, touched its lowest level since June in late January. The latest stock-market wobble has inspired only a modest bounce.

During the seven days ended Jan. 29, investors bought nearly $21 billion in U.S. equity funds, with much of that money flowing into technology-focused products as investors sought to buy the dip after Nvidia Corp.'s (NVDA) nearly $600 billion meltdown, according to data from EPFR.

That brought total inflows since the seven-day period ended Jan. 8 to $151.4 billion, on top of the $417.6 billion investors put to work in 2024, according to Winston Chua, a liquidity analyst at EPFR, a sister company of ISI Markets.

Taken together, these data have helped to explain the dominant dynamic that has prevailed in the U.S. market since the start of 2025, as things have turned more volatile.

Investors may get spooked from time to time. But so far, when markets have turned tumultuous, traders have been eager to buy the dip.

"People are optimistic and feeling good because we're in a bull market, and when you have some bad news, whether it be the AI stuff last week, or trade this week, and it is really amazing how quickly we go from pretty optimistic to downright petrified in a short time frame," said Ryan Detrick, chief market strategist at Carson Group.

"But that has kind of helped flush out the weak hands, and allowed buyers to step in." He added that he expects the S&P 500 to deliver a total return of between 12% and 15% in 2025.

If that happens, it would represent another above-average year for returns for the index after it delivered more than 25% returns back-to-back, including dividends.

There have been other signs that investors have been on edge in 2025. The Cboe Volatility Index VIX, otherwise known as the VIX or Wall Street's "fear gauge," had retreated to 15.77 as of Wednesday's close, after briefly topping 20 during the tariff-inspired selloff earlier this week.

Trading volume in VIX calls, which would likely pay off if the S&P 500 experienced a sudden drop, has been high, however, FactSet data showed. That could mean investors have been careful to hedge their bets, as high valuations relative to history have left stocks more vulnerable to a pullback.

Breadth makes a comeback

Another encouraging sign: After waning in late 2024, stock-market breadth has come roaring back since the start of the new year. The information technology sector, long one of the market's top performers, has struggled. But shares of companies in other corners of the market have helped to pick up the slack.

Since the new year began, all 10 of the other 11 sectors in the S&P 500 have traded in the green.

"The lifeblood of a bull market is rotation, passing that baton back and forth, it isn't just about technology now, in fact tech is down," Detrick said.

"You would think that might be a bad sign, yet, the market is doing just fine."

Even small-cap and midcap stocks, which had previously trailed their large-cap peers since the bull market began, have been climbing in 2025. The small-cap focused Russell 2000 RUT had gained nearly 4% in 2025 through Wednesday, surpassing the S&P 500.

This could mean investors are growing more confident in the strength of the underlying U.S. economy, and the ability of more companies to deliver strong earnings growth in 2025, Detrick said.

Burns McKinney, a portfolio manager at NFJ Investments, listed two other reasons for the stock market's strong performance.

"The two main things that investors are looking at is a Fed that remains in easing mode, even if they are slowing down the pace of rate cuts, and an administration that is focused on cutting taxes and easing regulations," McKinney said.

But at the same time, McKinney sees good reason to remain cautious. Valuations look stretched relative to history, and not just for Big Tech stocks.

U.S. stocks looked poised for another strong day on Thursday, with the S&P 500, the Dow Jones Industrial Average DJIA and the Nasdaq Composite COMP all on the cusp of record highs.

-Joseph Adinolfi

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  02-06-25 0939ET


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: National Heritage Academies Teachers Encourage Kids to Keep Moving During Winter Months
data.description:
National Heritage Academies Teachers Encourage Kids to Keep Moving During Winter Months

National Heritage Academies Teachers Encourage Kids to Keep Moving During Winter Months

PR Newswire

GRAND RAPIDS, Mich., Feb. 6, 2025

It may be cold outside, but that doesn't mean you have to become a 'couch potato.'

GRAND RAPIDS, Mich., Feb. 6, 2025 /PRNewswire/ -- A hot bowl of salty popcorn eaten while wrapped in a warm blanket can be a comforting remedy for a cold winter day. For students at Grand River Academy, popcorn is synonymous with moving during harsh conditions that make it easy to become a couch potato.

Hally Johns, the school's physical education teacher, runs wellness challenges each month that students take part in to stay active and create healthy habits, and they know they're on the right track when their heart is "popping like popcorn." To document their progress, students send in pictures and videos of themselves in a jumping jack challenge or a look at their dinnerplate to show they are staying healthy in the long months of winter. The winners are posted online along with the weekly wellness newsletter, incentivizing them to keep exercising. Johns said 50 to 75 students each month track their numbers.

"We talk about the difference between playing a video game and then actually working and strengthening your heart," Johns said. "Parents have been really great about checking in with me and making sure that I know they were doing it, so it's been cool."

Ready to enroll your student? National Heritage Academies operates 100 schools in nine states. Use our school finder to locate one near you: NHA School Finder.

Keeping children active is especially challenging during the cold winter months in Michigan, when outdoor time can be limited because of the lack of daylight or off the table when temperatures hover around zero degrees. Brad Agee, Cross Creek Charter Academy achievement and behavior support specialist and athletic director, combats the doldrums of winter by riding a stationary bike during his spare time. He stresses small increments of movement at minimum to himself and to his children.

"I told my kids, 'it's so much easier to do 10 minutes a day than to try to do 60 minutes on the weekend because you're going to lose half of what you gained in that time,'" he said. "If you can do 10 to 15 minutes a day of just something, especially if you're going to play a winter sport coming up, you're going to be way better off than saying, 'it's Sunday before we go back, I haven't done anything. I better go for a run real quick.' Then you're just going to be sore and miserable."

For parents seeking space for their kids to exert their pent up energy, Cross Creek offers open gym period so kids can play volleyball and basketball and also has indoor ultimate league with about 300 kids.

"We encourage the kids that are getting ready to come into sports to get some sort of activity going daily to make sure that they don't come into the first week of basketball and just fall on the floor because they've been sitting down for two weeks playing video games," Agee said.

Ridge Park Charter Academy Physical Education Teacher Steve Mercado stresses that if you do choose to get some fresh air while exercising in the winter, make sure to bundle up, take breaks, and hydrate. He offers some outdoor activities that can get the heartrate up for both kids and parents:

  • Snowball building
  • Snow tag
  • Fort building
  • Sledding (walking up the hill is a great workout)
  • Follow the leader
  • Shoveling (not as fun but great exercise)

Want to learn more about how NHA supports teachers and students to achieve at National Heritage Academies partner-schools? Visit nhaschools.com.

About National Heritage Academies:
National Heritage Academies (NHA) is a network of 100 tuition-free, public charter schools across nine states, serving more than 68,000 students in kindergarten through 12th grade. For more information, visit nhaschools.com. To find the nearest charter school near you, use the NHA school finder.

View original content to download multimedia:https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/national-heritage-academies-teachers-encourage-kids-to-keep-moving-during-winter-months-302370025.html

SOURCE National Heritage Academies

data.title: Adnoc Gas's Profit Beat Driven by Renegotiated Emirates Utility Contract — Update
data.description:
By Adam Whittaker 




  Adnoc Gas, one of the world's biggest natural-gas producers, reported a market beating fourth-quarter net income as it benefited from a renegotiated contract with the Emirates Water and Electricity Company. 



  The Abu Dhabi-owned gas company on Thursday said net profit for the quarter rose to $1.38 billion from $1.35 billion for the same period a year earlier, beating a $1.03 billion Visible Alpha consensus. 



  The company booked a $188 million credit in the period as it renegotiated a 10-year contract with EWEC, something that analysts hadn't factored into their calculations as the financial details hadn't been disclosed, Adnoc's chief financial officer Peter van Driel told Dow Jones Newswires. 



  Even without the one-off benefit, net profit for the quarter still beat expectations. 



  For the year as a whole Adnoc--which supplies 60% of the UAE's gas--reported a net income of $5 billion, up from $4.42 billion in 2023 as it benefited from higher domestic gas sales due to the temporary closure of the rival Dolphin pipeline. 



  The company said it is on track to meet its 40% earnings growth target from 2023 levels by 2029. 



  Meanwhile, the company expects to spend $15 billion on capital expenditure over the next five years, Driel said. 



  On Thursday Adnoc guided for 2025 capex spend between $3.0 billion and $3.5 billion, which will be concentrated on its IGD-E2, MERAM and Ruwais LNG facilities, and which will significantly enhance Adnoc's production capacity, Driel said. 



  Elsewhere on Thursday, Adnoc reported fourth-quarter total sales volumes of 869 trillion British thermal units. It said it expects 2025 domestic gas production between 2,320 trillion and 2,390 trillion British thermal units, compared with the 2,330 trillion BTU it produced in 2024. 





  Write to Adam Whittaker at adam.whittaker@wsj.com 





  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  February 06, 2025 09:28 ET (14:28 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: News Highlights: Top Company News of the Day - Thursday at 9 AM ET
data.description:
Honeywell to Break Up in Bid to Recreate Some GE Magic

One of America's last big industrial conglomerates is preparing for a three-way split, following General Electric and others. 

 
Conoco Quarterly  Net Income Fell, Expects 2025 Production Growth

Conoco Phillips said fourth-quarter net income declined alongside energy prices but the oil giant forecast production growth in 2025. 

 
Peloton Stock Surges on Revenue Beat and Guidance Boost

The company raises its fiscal-year forecast for adjusted Ebitda to a range of $300 million to $350 million. 

 
Bristol Myers Results Are a 'Tale of 2 Trends' as Guidance Disappoints

The drug company's fourth-quarter results soundly beat the Wall Street consensus. forecast 

 
AstraZeneca Expects More Sales, Earnings Growth After Results Beat Market Views

Strength in the pharmaceutical major's oncology business offset weak China sales, but the company has warned of a potential fine from a drug-import probe in the country. 

 
Philip Morris Revenue Tops Estimates as Zyn Demand Continues

Philip Morris International posted higher-than-expected revenue in its fourth quarter as its Zyn nicotine pouches continued to power results. 

 
ArcelorMittal Banks on Improved Steel Demand After Booking Narrowed Net Loss

The company said it believes demand for steel will improve this year after fewer impairment charges and other exceptional items led to a narrowed net loss in the last quarter of 2024. 

 
Eli Lilly Stock Rises After Earnings Beat, Upbeat Guidance

Fourth-quarter adjusted earnings of $5.32 a share beat analysts' estimates of $5.01. 

 
Coach Owner Tapestry Posts Earnings Beat and Raises Outlook. The Stock Skyrockets.

Tapestry's strong fiscal second quarter performance is driven largely by its Coach brand. 

 
Yum Brands' stock rises as Taco Bell and KFC fuel profit and revenue beats

data.title: News Highlights: Top Global Markets News of the Day - Thursday at 9 AM ET
data.description:
Bank of England Cuts Rates, Warns of Tariff Impact

The central bank responded to a troubling mix of stuttering economic growth, stubborn inflation and President Trump's tariff threats. 

 
Jobless claims rise slightly, but no sign of trouble brewing in labor market

The number of people who applied for unemployment benefits in early February rose slightly, but they remained at very low levels in a sign the labor market is still quite healthy. 

 
Dow, S&P 500 Futures Rise

Stock futures posted small moves, with Dow industrials and S&P 500 futures creeping higher. Markets rose in Asia and Europe. 

 
Eurozone Shoppers Spent Less as 2024 Ended Amid High Savings Caution

Retailers sold 0.2% less in December than November, a little worse than forecast, as consumers continued to save rather than splurge against a chilly economic backdrop. 

 
German Factory Orders Get Late-2024 Boost as Tariffs Loom

Manufacturing orders in Germany rose in December on aerospace demand, although any tentative signs of a recovery for the struggling industrial sector face the imminent threat of tariffs from the U.S. 

 
Hawkish BOJ Board Member Calls for Potentially Faster Rate Hikes

In a speech to business leaders, Naoki Tamura said that the central bank should raise rates to 1% or higher in the fiscal half starting in October. 

 
Power Stocks Had Been on an AI Tear. Then Came DeepSeek.

The efficiency gains of DeepSeek's AI models startled the power industry, which is set to invest billions in electricity projects. 

 
XRP Falls, Bitcoin Rises. The Crypto Tariff Fallout Is Mixed.

Altcoins haven't recovered fully from Monday's selloff. 

 
Private Equity's Lucrative Insurance Play in Pensions Challenged by Class-Action Suits

New lawsuits are asking the courts to decide a question policymakers have been debating-without resolution-for years: whether private equity is too risky to play a role in Americans' retirements. 

 
Trump and Musk's Actions Amount to Significant Pullback on Financial Regulation

Agencies overseeing consumer financial affairs, wealth managers, and commodity traders all seem to be scaling back oversight activity.

(END) Dow Jones Newswires

February 06, 2025 09:15 ET (14:15 GMT)

Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Yum Brands becomes latest fast-food chain to tap into AI
data.description:
By James Rogers

The company's newly announced AI offerings touch on areas like ordering and labor management

Shares of Yum Brands Inc. (YUM)rose 2.5% in premarket trading after the operator of the Pizza Hut, Taco Bell and KFC restaurant chains topped Wall Street's profit and revenue estimates, with the Taco Bell and KFC businesses fueling that outperformance.

The Louisville, Ky.-based company had net income of $423 million, or $1.49 a share, for the quarter, down from $463 million, or $1.62 a share, in the prior year's quarter.

The company also announced Thursday Byte by Yum!, a collection of software-as-a-service artificial intelligence-driven products for its restaurants. Byte by Yum! encompasses mobile app and web ordering, point-of-sale, kitchen and delivery optimization, menu management, inventory and labor management, and team-member tools, Yum Brands said. Currently, 25,000 Yum restaurants worldwide are using at least one Byte by Yum! product, the company added.

Other restaurant chains are harnessing AI. Rival Chipotle Mexican Grill Inc. (CMG), for example, is using AI to combat churn.

Yum's adjusted fourth-quarter profit was $1.61 a share, above the FactSet consensus estimate of $1.60 a share. Revenue rose to $2.362 billion from $2.036 billion, above the analyst revenue estimate of $2.356 billion.

Related: Yum Brands' stock falls after earnings miss with consumer still pressured

Yum Brands highlighted same-store-sales growth at Taco Bell U.S. and unit growth at KFC. "Our twin growth engines remain strong with Taco Bell U.S. delivering same-store sales growth of 5% in the fourth quarter, meaningfully outpacing the industry, and KFC International delivering its second consecutive year with over 2,000 net new units," Yum Brands Chief Executive David Gibbs said in a statement.

The KFC division opened 1,100 new restaurants during the fourth quarter.

"In 2024, we opened 4,535 new stores across more than 100 countries," CFO Chris Turner said. "Our digital progress this year was similarly enviable, with digital sales up approximately 15% and digital mix surpassing 50%, reflecting steady progress towards our ambition to reach 100% digital sales," he added.

Related: Taco Bell's success boosts Yum Brands' stock, as Pizza Hut and KFC see weakness

Separately, Yum Brands also announced a 6% increase in the company's quarterly dividend to 71 cents a share Thursday.

Related: Chipotle's sales have been 'volatile.' Can new menu items, kitchen upgrades and AI smooth things out?

Yum Brands shares have risen 0.7% in the last 12 months, compared with the S&P 500 Index's SPX gain of 21.4%.

-James Rogers

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  02-06-25 0905ET


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: China News Network Reports: Treasures in Yiwu New Year
data.description:
BEIJING, Feb. 06, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) -- As the world celebrates Chinese New Year, China News Network reports that the festive spirit spreads across oceans with goods from Yiwu, a vibrant city in East China's Zhejiang Province. 

A hub of commerce, Yiwu offers everything you want, from daily essentials to rare delights, such as bespoke jewelry and unique household decor.

It is also a tourist paradise, rich in culture, with its name "Yi" symbolizing integrity, a principle embraced by its business people.

The AI-composed track "Treasures in Yiwu New Year" invites you to experience its unique flavor.

PR Contact Name: Wen, Tianxia
Email: guojixinwenbu@chinanews.com.cn

A video accompanying this announcement is available at https://www.globenewswire.com/NewsRoom/AttachmentNg/caf85b0a-eae5-4720-bb45-37c323881380
